segments.s227-g97: دل (subject) + اندر (preposition) introduces the location phrase 'in/inside'. سرای سپنجی is a noun phrase 'house/room (سرای) described as spongy (سپنجی)'; مبند is a negative imperative (مـ- prefix + بند, classical negative command) meaning 'do not bind/close'. Word order is topical: 'heart' fronted, then the locative phrase, then the command.
segments.s227-g98: که introduces an explanatory subordinate clause ('for/because' here). پر خون شوی is a predicate with پر = 'full (of)' modifying خون and شوی (شد‑form) = 'become' (become full of blood). چون here functions as a temporal/conditional 'when' (classical use), followed by ببایدت کند: ببایدت is a modal idiom 'it is necessary for you / you must' with the light verb کند added for tense/voice — literally 'it will make (it necessary for) you'; together the phrase expresses 'when necessity/must comes upon you'.
segments.s227-g99: اگر introduces a protasis 'if'. یار is the subject 'companion/ally'. باشد is the literary/subjunctive/present form of 'to be' used here as 'is' or 'may be' (typical in conditional clauses). روان با خرد is a predicate phrase meaning 'whose spirit/mind is with wisdom' (روان = soul/spirit; با خرد = 'with wisdom' or 'wise'). The word order places the condition first, then its description.
segments.s227-g100: بنیک و ببد jointly modify روز (the coordinated adjectives 'the good and the bad [days]'). را is the definite/object marker marking روز as the object. بشمرد (subjunctive/present stem) here functions as a future/conditional result 'he will count/consider' — typical in classical Persian after a conditional clause.
segments.s227-g101: خداوند is an honorific/title 'the Lord' (here functioning as a divine or sovereign epithet) repeated for emphasis with two objects: تاج 'crown' and گنج 'treasure' (both in apposition). The repetition gives a list of attributes/titles.
segments.s227-g102: نبندد is a negative present/past-tense form meaning 'does not bind/close' (classical jussive/third‑person form). دل is the object 'heart'. اندر + سرای سپنج is the locative phrase 'inside a spongy house' (سرای here 'house/room' used with سپنج 'sponge'). The verb follows the subject phrase earlier (poetic inversion common in Ferdowsi).
segments.s227-g103: چنین 'thus/so' adverbially modifies the action. داد is the past tense 'gave' (here part of the verbal phrase 'gave an answer'). پاسخ is the direct object 'answer/response'. به رستم marks the beneficiary/indirect object 'to Rostam'. سپاه (army) is the agent; word order is inverted for poetic effect (object and verb before the subject).
segments.s227-g104: که introduces reported speech/explanation ('that'). فرمان تو is the noun phrase 'your command/order' (فرمان = command; تو = your). برتر از marks comparison 'higher/superior to'; چرخ ماه is a poetic compound 'the sky and the moon' (چرخ = heaven/sky/fate, ماه = moon), together emphasizing celestial loftiness — i.e. 'higher than heaven and moon'.
segments.s227-g57: ترا (ت + را) is the dative/object marker meaning 'to you' placed before the noun phrase for emphasis. پوشش از خود و چرم پلنگ is a noun phrase: پوشش 'a covering/cloak' modified by the genitive-like construction از خود 'of your own' and an apposition 'و چرم پلنگ' ('and leopard-skin'). Word order (dative → noun phrase) is emphatic and typical of poetic Persian.
segments.s227-g58: همی is an older/adverbial particle giving a habitual or emphatic sense ('still/indeed') that precedes the verb. خوشتر آید is a comparative predicate: خوش آمدن ('to please') in 3rd singular present (آید) with خوشتر 'more pleasing'. ز دیبای رنگ functions as the standard of comparison introduced by poetic ز ('than/from'), literally 'than the colored brocade/silk'.
segments.s227-g59: ندارد کسی inverts the usual word order (verb before noun) to express 'there is no one' or 'no one has' such a thing; here it means 'no one makes/holds'. با تو این داوری is the object/phrase 'this judgment about you' (با = 'with/regarding' you). The whole segment states that nobody utters this judgment concerning you.
segments.s227-g60: ز introduces the content/source of the alleged judgment ('that/from the seed...'). تخم پراکند is a noun phrase 'seed scattered' (پراکند as past participle/adjective 'scattered'). خود is 'your own'. بر خوری is the verb phrase: خوری is 2nd-person singular present/subjunctive of خوردن ('you eat'), preceded by بر which here functions as a prefatory particle (roughly 'up/from') forming the verbal phrase 'to eat of/consume'. Together it expresses 'that you eat (from) the seed you scattered yourself.'
segments.s227-g61: بدو is a prepositional/enclitic phrase 'to him' (بدو = به + او) indicating the indirect object. گفت here is the past-tense verb 'said' introducing reported speech; پیران is the agent 'the elders'. که‌ای is a compressed classical/exclamatory connector + vocative ('that — O ...' or simply the start of direct address) followed by نیکبخت 'fortunate' as a vocative. Word order places the addressee and exclamation right after the reporting verb.
segments.s227-g62: This is a string of attributive adjectives followed by the noun: برومند 'noble/prosperous', شاداب 'fresh/vigorous', زیبا 'beautiful', concluding with درخت 'tree' used metaphorically. In poetry adjectives may precede the noun for emphasis; here they form a laudatory vocative description of the addressee.
segments.s227-g63: سخنها is the plural subject 'words/utterances'. که introduces a relative clause 'who/that' and داند is 3rd-person singular present 'knows'. جز از تو 'except from you' restricts the agent: 'who knows such things other than you'. چنین 'such' modifies 'سخنها'. The construction stresses that no one besides the addressee knows such speech.
segments.s227-g64: که here introduces a wish/result clause: از مهتران 'from the elders' names the source, بر تو 'upon you' gives the target, باد is the optative particle 'may' used in classical Persian, and آفرین is 'praise' or 'acclamation'. The phrase literally reads 'may praise from the elders be upon you.'
segments.s227-g25: Literal order: subject (سپاهی) + verb (بیامد) + adverbial phrase (بدین سان) + source (ز چین). بیامد is the simple past 3rd-person singular ('came'). بدین سان is an adverbial phrase 'in this way/thus' built from the prefix ب- + دین (here not acting as an independent noun) + سان; ز before چین is the poetic preposition 'from'.
segments.s227-g26: A short series of places introduced by ز 'from' and joined by و 'and'. زمین follows توران to form 'توران زمین' = 'the land of Turan' (the noun زمین after a place name yields 'the land/country of X'). The list is simply several source-places for the arriving army.
segments.s227-g27: Interrogative + predicate + subject: کجا 'where' opens the question; آشتی is the noun 'peace/reconciliation' used predicatively; خواهد is the modal/auxiliary here ('will/would/wishes to') and the proper name افراسیاب appears as the subject at the end (typical poetic word order). The phrase asks where Afrasiab could/should make peace.
segments.s227-g28: که introduces a causal/explanatory clause ('for/since/that'). چندین modifies سپاه ('several/many hosts'); آمد is past tense 'came' (Ferdowsi often uses آمد with plural subjects); از خشک و آب is an idiom 'from land and sea' (خشک = the dry/land, آب = water/sea).
segments.s227-g29: بپاسخ is a prepositional/adverbial compound meaning 'in reply to' (ب- + پاسخ); نکوهش is the noun 'reproach/blame' and is the object of that phrase; بسی (an emphatic adverb) modifies یافتم ('I found/received') — یافتم is 1st-person past 'I found/obtained/was given.' The clause means 'I received much in answer (i.e. much reproach).'
segments.s227-g30: بدین سان is the same adverbial 'in this manner/thus' (ب- + دین + سان); سوی پهلوان is a directional phrase 'toward the champion/hero' (سوی = toward, پهلوان = hero); تافتم is 1st-person past of the archaic/poetic verb تافتن meaning here 'I turned/moved (toward)'. Verb comes at the end as typical in poetry.
segments.s227-g31: This is a simile: سپاهی 'host/army' is compared with a 'دریای آب' (a sea of water) using چو 'like/as'. وزیشان is a fused poetic element (literally 'وز ایشان' or similar) linking this clause to the preceding lines and indicating 'and/of them/their ...' (the exact morpheme is archaic/poetic).
segments.s227-g32: گرفتند is past 3rd-plural 'they took/seized' and governs the following nominal infinitive phrase. بر جنگ جستن is an infinitival phrase 'to seek/undertake war' (بر marks the object/aim, جنگ = war, جستن = 'to seek/attempt'); شتاب ('haste') is the thing they 'took' — i.e. 'they took haste to seek battle' = 'they hastened to engage in battle.'
segments.s227-g113: سنانهای نیزه is a genitive/construct sequence (literally “points of the spear”) with the possessive relation implied by word order; بگرد is a short/poetic verbal form meaning “turned/spun” (here acting as the predicate) and اندرون is an adverbial locative “within/inside,” so the segment reads literally “the spear‑points (of the) spear turned within.”
segments.s227-g114: ستاره (literally “star”) is an animate/figurative subject here; بیالود is an adjective following it (poetic adjective phrase, “unstained/pure”); گفتی is the verb form meaning “said/uttered” (the given lemma’s senses do not list the simple past meaning, so the exact dictionary senses supplied do not match — see senses mapping); بخون is an imperative “Sing!/Recite!” addressed to someone or uttered as a cry. The whole segment functions as direct speech/report: “the (pure) star said, ‘Sing!’.”
segments.s227-g115: چرنگیدن is an infinitive/verbal noun form meaning to move about/roam/whirl (a poetic motion verb); it bears the action and is followed by گرزه (the instrument “mace”), with گاوچهر as a following name in apposition — together: “Gavchehr’s mace (was) whirling.” The sequence omits auxiliary verbs as is common in poetry.
segments.s227-g116: تو is the 2nd‑person pronoun “you”; گفتی here functions as the reporting verb “you said” (the provided lemma senses list nouns like “conversation,” so the exact spoken‑verb sense isn’t represented in the supplied senses and is marked -1); همی is an emphatic/continuative adverb (“indeed/still”); سنگ is the direct object “stones”; بارد is a terse poetic form of a verb meaning “rained” (classical poetry often uses such terse forms), and سپهر is the locative/subject “the sky/heavens” — together: “you said, the heavens rained stones.”
segments.s227-g117: بخون is an imperative “Sing!/Recite!” addressing either a singer or issuing as a cry; the coordinating و links to بمغز which here functions adverbially/phrase‑like (‘to the marrow’ / ‘into the core’), followed by اندرون “within” as a locative; خار و خاک (“thorn and dust”) are two nouns joined by و describing the brutal contents or condition inside. The order places the imperative first, then a short verbless descriptive cluster describing internal states.
segments.s227-g118: شده is the perfect/passive participial form “has/become,” and غرق is the participial/verb meaning “drowned/immersed”; the coordinating و links the next clause; برگستوان here appears as a nominal/compound referring to the arms/shoulders (in this context treated as the locus of wounds) and چاک چاک is a doubled adjective/adverbial onomatopoeic phrase “torn, gaping” — together: “(they) have become drowned, and (their) arms (are) rent, rent.”
segments.s227-g119: همه دشت is a nominal phrase “the whole plain” (همه modifying دشت); یکسر is an adverb meaning “completely/entirely” modifying the following predicate; پر از is the adjectival phrase “full of” governed by از (“of/from”); جوی خون is a genitive group “streams of blood.” Word order follows Persian noun + modifier + predicate pattern: “the whole plain entirely full of streams of blood.”
segments.s227-g120: بهر جای is a locative phrase meaning “for/to every place” or “in every spot”; چندی here means “many/several” or “a number” (used adverbially/quantitatively); فگنده is a past participle/adjectival form “thrown/scattered/spilled” describing the people or bodies; نگون is a predicate adjective “overthrown/ill‑fated.” Together they state the scene: “in every place many were thrown down, overthrown.”
segments.s227-g89: دو دستش = numeral + noun + enclitic pronoun: 'دو' (two) precedes the noun 'دست' (hand), and the enclitic 'ش' marks 'his'. ببندم is a first‑person singular present/subjunctive form of بستن used here to express the speaker's intent or determination ('I will bind' / 'I shall bind'). بخم کمند is a compound phrase where بخم is an archaic/contracted form of به + خم ('to/with the loop/bend') and کمند is the instrument 'snare, lasso' — together 'with the loop of the snare'.
segments.s227-g90: اگر introduces a conditional or concessive clause ('if' / 'even if'). یار here means 'ally/companion' in the sense of a supporting power. باشد is the subjunctive/present of بودن used for hypothetical concession ('there be' / 'he have'). سپهر بلند is a noun + adjective ('the high/lofty sky') used metaphorically to indicate an extremely powerful or lofty ally.
segments.s227-g91: شما is the second‑person plural/plural‑polite 'you'. سربسر is an adverb meaning 'entirely' or 'throughout' and modifies the following predicate. یک بیک همگروه is a noun phrase: یک (one) + بیک (bey, chieftain) and همگروه ('fellow‑member' / 'companion') — together 'companions of a single bey'. Word order is emphatic: 'you, all together, (do not) be companions of one bey.'
segments.s227-g92: مباشید is the negative imperative/optative form 'do not be' addressed to plural 'you'. از here functions as 'because of / by' marking the cause or source. آن نامداران ('those renowned ones') is the object of that preposition, and ستوه ('weary / sick of') is a predicate adjective: 'be not weary because of those famed men.'
segments.s227-g93: مرا‌گر is a fused conditional 'مرا گر' meaning 'if (it) be for me / if it befall me'. برزم carries a possessive -م ('my') and here refers to 'battle / the fighting that concerns me' (classical poetic use); اندر آید زمان is a subordinate clause 'the time comes in / the time arrives' with آید (present/optative of آمدن) 'comes'. The overall sense: 'If the time for my battle comes...'.
segments.s227-g94: نمیرم is first‑person singular negative of مردن used here to state a firm resolution 'I will not die'. ببزم is a contracted/poetic locution built on بزم ('feast/assembly'); its exact lexical senses are archaic/ambiguous in the dictionary evidence given, so I mark it as not directly matched below. اندرون is the adverb 'inside / within' and بی‌گمان is an adverbial 'undoubtedly / surely' qualifying the whole clause.
segments.s227-g95: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle ('always/indeed') modifying the statement. نام here means 'name' in the sense of reputation or fame. باید is a modal 'must/ought' expressing necessity. که ماند دراز is a relative/subordinate clause 'that it remain long' where ماند is present/subjunctive 'remain' and دراز is 'long' — together 'a name must endure long.'
segments.s227-g96: نمانی is a negative optative/imperative form meaning 'do not remain / do not seek to last'. همی again adds emphasis. کار چندین ('many deeds' / 'numerous actions') is the noun phrase headed by کار ('deed, affair') modified by چندین ('several, many a'). مساز is the negative imperative 'do not make / do not contrive' — together the line warns against trying to secure lastingness by accumulating many deeds.
segments.s227-g1: وزان is a fused classical phrase meaning 'from that' (به + آن) marking origin; جایگه is a noun 'place'. شد is the past simple verb 'became/was' used here intransitively to mark movement/arrival (classical usage where 'شد' often indicates what happened). ب + دان here together form the demonstrative 'to that' (به + آن) pointing to 'انجمن' (the assembly). The whole chunk states origin and arrival at that assembly.
segments.s227-g2: بجایی که is a directional phrase 'to the place that...' (بِ + جایی + که). که introduces a relative clause. سایه is 'shadow' (or 'shade') and پیلتن is a place-name or topographical term; the clause specifies which place — the one in Piltan's shade. Note some small classical contractions/spellings make exact segmentation ambiguous (see senses).
segments.s227-g3: فرود آمد is a compound verb meaning 'he descended/alighted' (فرود = descent, آمد = came). و is the coordinating 'and'. آفرین کرد is a light-verb construction: آفرین (acclamation/praise) + کرد ('did/made') = 'he praised' or 'he offered praise'. چند here is an adverb 'a little/few' modifying the praise.
segments.s227-g4: که introduces reported content 'that...'. زور 'strength' is the subject of گیرد; از تو 'from you' marks source, with گرفتن (گیرد) in the present/subjunctive sense 'may take/take from'. سپهر بلند 'the lofty heaven' is the place/agent taking strength — an elevated, praising formula meaning 'may heaven draw strength from you.'
segments.s227-g5: مبادا is an optative/negative-jussive particle 'may it not be / lest'. که introduces the clause. روز تو 'your day' = your fortune/turn; گیرد (from گرفتن) is used causatively/impersonal 'take/seize' in present/subjunctive sense. نشیب 'descent/decline' is the result; together the clause is a wish forbidding a downturn of fortune.
segments.s227-g6: Again مبادا introduces a negative wish. آید is present/subjunctive 'may come'. برویت is an older or contracted locution meaning 'upon your face / before you' (بِ + رویت = to/onto your face) and functions as the indirect object of نهیب. نهیب 'rebuke' (or 'thundering call') is the thing wished away. Grammatically: 'May a rebuke not come upon you.'
segments.s227-g7: دل شاه ایران is a possessive noun phrase 'the heart of the Shah of Iran'. بتو is the prepositional phrase 'toward you / for you'. شاد is an adjective 'happy/joyful'. باد is the optative particle 'may' (classical wish marker). The clause is a conventional benediction: 'May the Shah of Iran's heart be glad about you.'
segments.s227-g8: همه کار تو = 'all your affairs'. سربسر is an adverbial 'altogether/entirely'. داد here is a nominal sense 'justice' (a classical wish-formula where 'داد باد' expresses a wish for rightful order), and باد again is the optative 'may'. The line prays that all the addressee's affairs be entirely in/with justice (i.e. go well/rightly).
segments.s227-g49: چنین is a premodifier meaning 'such' placed before the noun زندگانی ('life'); نیارد is a classical poetic form meaning 'does/does not bring' or 'yields' (here negative idea: 'brings no'); بها is the object 'value/worth'. Word order is adjective + noun + verb + object, a typical classical Persian ordering where the verb follows its noun phrase.
segments.s227-g50: که introduces an explanatory/subordinate clause ('for/that'); باشد is the classical present/subjunctive form of 'to be' (here stating the (undesirable) condition); سر is the subject 'head' and اندر دم اژدها is a prepositional phrase 'in/within the dragon's breath/mouth' (اندر = 'in', دم used here in the sense of 'breath, mouth/edge' in poetic usage). The clause explains why such a life lacks value.
segments.s227-g51: مگر here functions as a rhetorical particle (roughly 'did I not...' / 'surely...'); گفتم is the past tense 'I said' (the given sense list does not show this basic meaning); آن خاک is 'that soil/land' and بیداد و شوم are postpositive adjectives, 'injustice/oppressive' and 'ill‑omened/accursed' joined by و. The whole phrase reports what the speaker claims to have said about the land.
segments.s227-g52: گذاری is used here as a modal/lexical verb meaning roughly 'to allow / to cause' (classical usages vary between 'place' and 'let'); بیایی is a present/subjunctive/optative form 'you come' (2nd person), so the two verbs form a construction about permitting or resulting in someone coming. به آباد بوم is the prepositional phrase 'to the inhabited/prosperous land' (به = 'to', آباد = 'inhabited/prosperous', بوم = 'land/soil').
segments.s227-g53: ببینی is a present/subjunctive used as a conditional 'if you see' or 'should you see'; مگر is a particle here (classical poetic use gives a shading of 'perhaps' / rhetorical emphasis); شاه is the subject 'the king'; با داد و مهر is an instrumental/qualifying phrase 'with justice and affection' (داد = 'justice', مهر = 'affection/kindness'). Word order puts the verb (ببینی) up front as a protasis.
segments.s227-g54: This is a coordinated noun phrase giving those who accompany or are before the king: جوان 'young (people)', نوازنده 'musicians' (the instrumental/performative kind), and خوب‌چهر 'fair‑faced' (an adjective used substantively). Each item is joined by و. The phrase completes the picture introduced by 'شاه با داد و مهر'.
segments.s227-g55: بدارد is a classical third‑person verb (from داشتن/برداشتن family) meaning 'he takes/keeps/holds' or 'treats (someone) as'; ترا is the object formed from the clitic ت ('you') plus the object marker را; چون پدر is a simile 'as a father'; بی‌گمان is an adverb 'undoubtedly'. The clause expresses assurance about how he will treat you.
segments.s227-g56: برآرد is a third‑person verb meaning 'raises / brings up / lifts' (here causative/resultative); سرت is 'your head' (سر + 2nd‑person clitic); برتر از آسمان is a comparative phrase 'higher/superior than the sky' (برتر = 'higher/better', از = 'than/from', آسمان = 'sky/heaven'). The clause states the elevated result (honor/glory) given to you.
segments.s227-g9: برفتم is a past first-person verb meaning "I departed/left" (the prefix بر- appears in poetic diction); ز نزد تو is a prepositional expression "from your presence/near you" with ز + نزد. ای پهلوان is a vocative: ای introduces direct address and پهلوان is the addressee "O champion."
segments.s227-g10: پیامت is پیام + enclitic -ت (your message) as the direct object. بدادم here functions as a past-tense verb "I gave/delivered" (بـ + دادم). بپیر و جوان is a prepositional phrase (poetic contraction of به پیر و جوان) meaning "to old and young," with و joining the two groups.
segments.s227-g11: بگفتم is a past first-person verb "I said/I related" (the b- prefix is common in classical Persian verbs). هنرهای تو is the plural noun "your skills/arts/merits." هرچ بود is a relative clause "whatever there was," where هرچ = "whatever" and بود is the past copula "was."
segments.s227-g12: بگیتی is an adverbial phrase meaning "among people / in the world." ترا is the object pronoun (here shown split as ت + را) meaning "you." خود is emphatic/reflexive "myself/very;" که introduces a subordinate clause "that/which," and یارد ستود is a clause "the companion (یا رَد) praised" with ستود the past verb "praised." Overall: 'I told the world of you myself, that even a companion praised you.'
segments.s227-g13: هم ... هم ... is the correlative pair "both ... and ...". از آشتی راندم literally has از + آشتی "from/ concerning reconciliation" and راندم is past "I drove (away) / I put aside"; هم ز جنگ adds "and (from) war" (ز = from/of, جنگ = war). The clause contrasts the two matters being addressed or repelled.
segments.s227-g14: سخن گفتم is literally "I spoke words / I spoke" with سخن "speech/words" and گفتم the past verb "I said/spoke." از هر دری is an idiomatic phrase "about every (kind of) matter" (هر = every, دری here = 'door/quarter/topic' in the idiomatic sense), and بی‌درنگ is an adverb "without delay / immediately."
segments.s227-g15: بفرجام is an adverb meaning "at last / toward the end." گفتند is past plural "they said." کین is the object "this vengeance/hatred," and چون کنيم is a question clause "how shall we do?/how shall we act?" with کنيم the present/subjunctive/optative first-person-plural form used for 'shall we do'.
segments.s227-g16: که introduces the purpose/result clause "so that/that." از رای او is a prepositional phrase "from his counsel/opinion" (از = from, رای = counsel/plan/opinion, او = he/his). کینه بیرون کنیم is the verb phrase "we remove/drive out hatred," where بیرون = out/away and کنیم is the 1st-person-plural subjunctive/present of "to do" used here for 'we may do/remove.'
segments.s227-g73: چنین (thus) is a sentence adverb placed first for emphasis. گفت is the finite verb 'said' and is followed by the speaker رستم as subject; بایرانیان is the addressed party (indirect object) with no preposition — classical Persian often omits 'to' before the addressee. The whole segment = 'Thus Rostam said to the Iranians.'
segments.s227-g74: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that'). من is the first-person pronoun 'I'. جنگ را uses را as the definite/direct-object marker marking 'the battle/fighting' as the object. بسته دارم is a present-tense construction: بسته (participle/adjective 'closed/contained') + دارم ('I have/hold'), here meaning 'I hold/keep (it) closed/contained'. میان ('between') is a prepositional/adverbial particle hanging at the end to indicate location/limit (elliptically 'between us/you/them').
segments.s227-g75: شما ('you', vocative/imperative addressee) is the agent of the imperative. یک بیک ('one bey/chieftain') is the direct object introduced without a determiner. سر پر از کین is an attributive phrase ('a head full of hatred') using سر (head) + پر از ('full of') + کین (hatred/vengeance) to qualify the bey. کنید is the 2nd-person-plural imperative/subjunctive 'do/make' placed at the end and governs the whole clause.
segments.s227-g76: This is a parallel imperative clause sharing the same verb sense 'کنید' (make/let). بروهای is a plural agentive/participle ('those who go' / 'goers', here 'fighters who go out to battle'). جنگی is a postpositive adjective 'warlike/military' modifying بروهای. پر از چین again is 'full of wrinkles/furrows' (چین = wrinkle/furrow) — here figuratively 'grim brows' — and کنید closes the imperative.
segments.s227-g77: که ('that') introduces the content clause. امروز ('today') is a temporal adverb. رزمی ('martial') functions as a substantive here meaning 'a battle/contest' and بزرگست is the fused poetic form of بزرگ است ('is great'); پیش ('before/ahead') indicates that this great combat lies in front/approaching.
segments.s227-g78: پدید آید is a verbal phrase (پدید آمدن 'to appear, arise'); آید is third-person singular present/future 'will come/arise' so together 'will appear/arise.' اندازه here introduces a measure/time idiom ('at the measure/degree of'), and گرگ و میش ('wolf and ewe') is an idiomatic expression meaning the dim light of dawn/dusk; the phrase thus says the battle will appear at that twilight hour.
segments.s227-g79: مرا is the accusative pronoun 'me' (object). گفته بود is the past-perfect/pluperfect compound (past participle گفته + auxiliary بود) meaning 'had said/told'. آن ستاره‌شناس is the postposed subject 'that astronomer/astrologer' (in narration the subject can follow the verb), so the clause means 'that astronomer had told me.'
segments.s227-g80: ازین روز ('from this day' = since that day) is formed from از ('from') + این (here written as ین) + روز ('day'). بودم is first-person past 'I was.' دل اندر هراس is an idiomatic locution ('heart in fear' = 'fearful at heart'), where اندر = 'in/within' and هراس = 'fear/terror'. Together: 'From that day I was fearful in my heart.'
segments.s227-g65: مرا جان و دل زیر فرمان تست is a compact predication meaning “my life and heart are under your command.” مرا is the older/dative-style possessive/object-marker standing before جان و دل to mark them as belonging to ‘me’ (lit. “to me, life and heart”). زیر فرمان is a prepositional phrase “under (the) command,” and تست is the archaic second-person copula/enclitic (تو + است) linking the predicate to ‘you’ (i.e. ‘they are yours’). Word order places the possessor/affected phrase up front for emphasis, then the location/condition phrase and the copula.
segments.s227-g66: همیشه روانم گروگان تست continues the thought with an adverbial modifier and a nominal predicate: همیشه (“always/forever”) modifies the whole clause; روانم is ‘my spirit/soul’ (روان + possessive suffix -م). گروگان is a predicative noun ‘hostage / pledge’, and تست again is the archaic second-person copula indicating that the speaker’s spirit is (held) by the addressee. The clause thus reads literally ‘always my spirit (is) a hostage (to) you.’
segments.s227-g67: یک امشب زنم رای با خویشتن: یک امشب is an adverbial phrase meaning roughly ‘for one night / for a moment tonight’ (a short time tonight). رای زدن is an idiomatic verbal compound meaning ‘to take counsel / deliberate’; زنم is the first-person form of زدن used in that compound (‘I will take/cast counsel’). با خویشتن is a reflexive prepositional phrase ‘with myself.’ Overall: ‘Tonight I will take counsel with myself (for a while).’
segments.s227-g68: بگویم سخن نیز با انجمن: بگویم is the first-person singular verb ‘I say / I will say’ (classical/subjunctive form often used to indicate a future or deliberate action). سخن is the object ‘speech / a word,’ and نیز ‘also’ links this action to the previous one. با انجمن = ‘with the assembly/meeting.’ The clause therefore means ‘I will also speak with the assembly.’
segments.s227-g69: وزانجا بیامد بقلب سیاه: وزانجا = و + آنجا ‘and from there.’ بیامد is simple past ‘came.’ بقلب is the prepositional phrase ‘into (the) heart’ and سیاه is an adjectival postmodifier ‘black’ (here with the connotation ‘dark/evil’). Word order is typical poetic Persian: source (from there) + verb + target (into the heart) + adjective.
segments.s227-g70: زبان پر دروغ و روان کینه‌خواه: this is a coordinated noun phrase. زبان here is used figuratively = ‘tongue / speech / utterance.’ پر (preposed) means ‘full (of),’ so زبان پر دروغ = ‘a tongue full of lies.’ و links the second coordinated phrase روان کینه‌خواه, where روان = ‘soul/spirit’ and کینه‌خواه = ‘vengeful’ (an adjectival/agentive compound). So: ‘a lying tongue and a vengeful spirit.’
segments.s227-g71: چو برگشت پیران ز هر دو گروه: چو is the conjunction ‘when.’ برگشت is the past (perfect) verb ‘returned’ (3rd person), and پیران is the plural subject ‘the elders’ (placed after the verb in this poetic order). ز = ‘from,’ and هر دو گروه = ‘both groups/bands.’ The clause reads ‘when the elders returned from both groups.’
segments.s227-g72: زمین شد بکردار جوشنده کوه: زمین ‘the earth/ground’ is the subject, شد is the past of شدن ‘became,’ and the remainder is a noun phrase in predicative/transformational position: بکردار (literally ‘with/characterized by deeds’ → ‘virtuous’ or ‘full of acts’) and جوشنده کوه (‘a seething/boiling mountain’). Poetically: ‘the earth became, by its deeds, a seething mountain,’ i.e. the land was stirred like a boiling mountain. Note the copular شدن + noun phrase construction common in classical Persian.
segments.s227-g81: که is the relative/conjunctive 'that' introducing the clause. رزمی بود is a predicate: رزمی (martial/combat) + بود (past of 'to be') = 'there was a martial fight'. در میان دو کوه is a prepositional phrase ('in/between two mountains') with the head noun کوه preceded by the numeral دو.
segments.s227-g82: جهانی is used as a substantive/adjective referring to the peoples or the world at large; شوند (third person plural) is the present/subjunctive/optative form 'become/will be' used narratively to mean 'will come together'. اندر آن همگروه is an archaic/prepositional phrase 'in/among that company' (اندر = 'in', آن = 'that', همگروه = 'of the same group / companions').
segments.s227-g83: شوند precedes the noun phrase (poetic inversion) and means 'will become/shall be formed'. انجمن is the noun 'assembly/gathering'. کاردیده is an adjective 'battle‑tested, experienced' modifying مهان ('guests' / visitors, here likely 'veteran warriors' or honored guests). The whole phrase = 'an assembly of battle‑tested guests will gather.'
segments.s227-g84: بدان is a fused classical/conjunctive form meaning 'by/with/because of that' (ب + آن); جنگ is the noun 'war' and بی‌مرد is an adjective 'devoid of men / manless' (poetically 'bereft of people'). گردد (گردد/گشت) is the verbal predicate 'becomes / will become' with جهان ('the world') as the affected domain: 'by that war the world becomes manless.'
segments.s227-g85: پی here means 'after / following / in pursuit of' and takes کین نهان ('hidden grudge / secret hatred') as its object. گردد is the verbal form 'will happen / will arise'. از روی بوم is a locative phrase 'from the face/surface of the bōm' (بوم = homeland/soil), so the clause means 'after that hidden hatred there will arise from the surface of the land.'
segments.s227-g86: شود is another third‑person form 'it will be / will happen'. گرز پولاد is a noun phrase 'the steel mace' (گرز = mace, پولاد = steel). برسان here is used in the lexical sense 'make/bring to' or 'render' and موم (wax) is the result image—poetically implying the steel mace will be rendered like wax (i.e., easily melted/broken or powerless). The word order is poetic and condensed: verb + instrument + result.
segments.s227-g87: هر آنکس که is the standard relative phrase 'whoever (that)'. آید is the present/subjunctive 'comes'; بر ما is the prepositional 'against us'; بجنگ functions as an infinitival/nominalized verb 'to fight' (in poetry the infinitive can follow or be condensed), so the clause reads 'whoever comes to fight against us.'
segments.s227-g88: شما is the vocative/plural 'you'. دل is the object 'heart' (i.e., emotions). مدارید is a present form of an older verb used here as an exhortation ('do not be distressed / do not let your hearts be moved' — the exact shading is idiomatic and negative/prohibitory in context). از آن کار تنگ is a prepositional phrase 'by/over that narrow/tight affair' (تنگ = close/narrow, here 'difficult, constrained' affair). The whole is an address: 'you, do not let your hearts be troubled by that narrow matter.'
segments.s227-g105: چنان is an adverb meaning 'so/thus' (here 'such/so'); رزم (object) precedes the verb سازیم, a first-person-plural subjunctive/optative form meaning 'let us make/prepare' or 'we will make' in poetic diction. با تیغ تیز is a prepositional phrase 'with a sharp sword(s)' where تیغ (noun) is modified by the adjective تیز which follows it in normal Persian order.
segments.s227-g106: که introduces a result/relative clause 'that/so that'; ماند is a verb 'remain/last' (here third person singular, subjunctive/optative sense: 'may remain' / 'will remain'); ز ما نام = 'a name/of us' (ز functions poetically as 'of/from' for possession); تا = 'until' and رستخیز = 'Resurrection' (Day of Rising). Word order is slightly inverted for poetry: object ('نام') appears after the prepositional phrase before the verb.
segments.s227-g107: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; دو رویه literally 'two faces/sides/approaches' (رویه = side/surface) with the numeral preceding the noun; تنگ 'narrow' postmodifies رویه. اندر آمد is the compound past 'came in/entered' and سپاه (the army/host) is the noun that performs the action, placed after the verb for poetic inversion.
segments.s227-g108: یکی ابر = 'a single/a certain cloud' (یکی = one/a certain) and سیاه = 'black/dark' modifies ابر. گفتی here is a small archaic/poetic parenthetical particle meaning 'so to speak' or 'as if' (not the ordinary verb 'said'); برآمد is the verb 'arose/issued forth'. The phrase therefore reads 'a single dark cloud, as it were, arose.'
segments.s227-g109: که here links back as 'for/that/whose' (a relative/resultive link). باران او = 'his/its rain' (او refers to the cloud just mentioned); بود is the past copula 'was'; شمشیر و تیر are the predicate nominatives 'sword and arrow', so the line means 'whose rain was sword and arrow' (i.e. the cloud's rain was swords and arrows).
segments.s227-g110: جهان شد = 'the world became' (شد = became). بکردار here is an archaic/poetic word functioning as a predicative modifier meaning roughly 'in the state/guise of' or 'turned into' (not the ordinary sense 'virtuous' in modern lists); دریای قیر = 'a sea of tar' (دریای = genitive/attributive 'sea of', قیر = 'tar'). Together: 'the world became (as) a sea of tar.'
segments.s227-g111: ز = 'by/from'; پیکان پولاد is a noun + modifier meaning 'steel (piled) arrow' or 'steel‑headed arrows' (پولاد postmodifies پیکان); و connects the coordinated item پر عقاب 'feather of the eagle' (پر = feather, عقاب = eagle). Together they indicate the instruments/agents producing the effect.
segments.s227-g112: سیه گشت = 'became black/dark' (سیه 'black' followed by the past 'became' گشت). رخشان رخ آفتاب = 'the shining/radiant face of the sun' where رخشان (radiant) modifies رخ (face) and آفتاب specifies whose face (the sun). The adjective + noun group follows the verb for poetic inversion: 'the sun's shining face became dark.'
segments.s227-g33: نبرد تو خواهد همی شاه هند is arranged in a somewhat archaic/poetic order: نبرد here functions as the action 'to fight / do battle' and is followed by its object تو ('you'); خواهد همی is the older/fixed future/auxiliary construction (خواهد = 'will') with همی adding emphasis ('indeed/always'); شاه هند is the logical subject placed at the end ('the king of Hind'). So the clause means 'the king of Hind will (indeed) fight you.'
segments.s227-g34: بتیر و کمان و بهندی پرند lists instruments and actors: بتیر و کمان ('with arrows and bow(s)') uses juxtaposition without an explicit preposition (typical poetic coordination), and بهندی پرند places the adjective/ethnic label بهندی ('Indian/of Hind') before پرند. Here پرند is best read as a verb 'they fly/rush' (3rd person plural ending -ند) referring back to the Indians; so the phrase means 'with bows and arrows the Indians rush forth.'
segments.s227-g35: مرا این درستست کز پیلتن is a clause meaning 'this is true to me that ...'. مرا is the experiencer/ethical dative ('to me' / 'I know/feel'), این درستست is a fixed predication 'this is true/certain', and کز introduces the subordinate clause ('that' / 'from/ because of'), here linking the following phrase about پیلتن (a place/name).
segments.s227-g36: بفرجام گریان شوند انجمن: بفرجام ('at the end' / 'in the end') is an adverbial phrase; گریان is a participial/adjectival form 'weeping, tearful'; شوند is the 3rd-person plural present/future of شدن 'become' (here 'will be'); انجمن is the subject placed after the verb (poetic order). Together: 'the assembly will in the end become weeping.'
segments.s227-g37: چو بشنید رستم برآشفت سخت: چو = 'when' introduces a temporal clause; بشنید is the past tense 'he heard'; رستم is the subject; برآشفت is a past form with the prefix بر- meaning 'rose up/was roused'—here 'grew angry'; سخت is an adverb/adjective intensifier 'severely/very'. Word order is subject after the verb cluster, typical of classical poetry.
segments.s227-g38: بپیران چنین گفت کای شوربخت: بپیران (= به پیران) means 'to the elders' or 'before the elders' (the prepositional/instrumental use be- + plural پیران); چنین گفت = 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = 'said'); کای is the vocative particle 'O' and شوربخت 'unfortunate/wretched' is the addressee label. So: 'to the elders he said thus: "O wretch..."'.
segments.s227-g39: تو با این‌چنین بند و چندین فریب: تو 'you' is the addressee; با + noun phrase 'with these/with such' (با این‌چنین) marks accompaniment or means; بند here is 'bond/fetter/tie' (poetic for trickery or ties), and چندین فریب 'many deceptions' further lists the vices/means. This is an accusative/adjunct phrase describing what the addressee relies on.
segments.s227-g40: کجا پای داری بروز نهیب is a rhetorical question: کجا 'where', پای داری literally 'do you have a foot' i.e. 'where will you stand / what footing will you have'; بروز is the prepositional/time phrase 'on the day (of...)' and نهیب is the noun 'rebuke / denunciation' (here 'the day of rebuke' or 'day of reckoning'). The verb داری is present 'you have', so together: 'where will you have footing on the day of rebuke?'
segments.s227-g17: توان here indicates ability ('can') and is followed by داد (the verb 'to give' in its simple form), so together they express the power to give. The list that follows (گنج و زر و خواسته) is the direct object: treasure, gold, and 'the desired/requested thing'. Word order is typical Persian: modal/auxiliary + verb + objects.
segments.s227-g18: ز ما (from us) marks the source; هر چه او خواهد is a free relative ('whatever he wants') with هر ... چه = 'whatever' and خواهد in the present-tense sense 'wishes/wants'. آراسته is a predicative adjective/participle meaning 'adorned/furnished' and here completes the clause: 'whatever he wants from us is furnished.'
segments.s227-g19: نشاید is an impersonal present tense meaning 'it is not fitting' or 'one should not.' گنهکار دادن uses دادن ('to give') in the sense 'to give the epithet / to pronounce (as) guilty' and بدوی functions adverbially ('at first' / 'prematurely' — here from the sense 'primitive/original' used to mean 'initially'). The whole segment says one should not hastily accuse someone of guilt.
segments.s227-g20: براندیش is an imperative or hortative meaning 'consider / think it over.' و links the two imperatives. این رازها is the object 'these secrets,' and بازجوی here is an imperative 'investigate / inquire into' (the listed noun senses point to an investigator; in context this is a verbal command).
segments.s227-g21: This is an identification: گنهکار 'the guilty one' + جز خویش ش 'except his own' (جز = 'except'; خوی + ش is the old-fashioned construction for 'his own') followed by the name افراسیاب. The sense is 'the guilty one, besides himself, is Afrasiab' — idiomatically: 'the guilty one is Afrasiab himself.'
segments.s227-g22: که introduces a clause of explanation ('since' / 'for that which'); دانی is 2nd-person present 'you know.' سخن را (را marks the definite object 'the matter/word') and مزن is a negative imperative 'do not strike/utter' used idiomatically here as 'do not say/declare.' در شتاب 'in haste' (در + شتاب) qualifies the verb: 'do not state it hastily.'
segments.s227-g23: ز ما 'from us' sets the domain. هرک (every/whomever) with خواهد ('he wants/chooses') forms 'whoever he wants from among us.' همه مهترند is the main predication: 'all are mahters' (chiefs/leaders). The verb مهترند is a plural copula-marked predicate ('they are chiefs').
segments.s227-g24: بزرگند 'they are great' is a second predicate joined by و 'and'. با تخت و با افسرند uses با + noun twice ('with throne and with helm') and the final افسرند is a plural copula construction meaning 'they are with helms' or 'they wear helms' (افسر here denotes helmet). Together: 'they are great and come with throne and helmet.'
segments.s227-g121: چو introduces a simile ('like'). پیلان is the plural of پیل (elephant) and is the simile's head noun. فگنده is a past participle/adjectival form meaning 'thrown' or 'cast down' modifying پیلان. بهم indicates mutual togetherness ('to/onto one another'), and the doubled میل میل is a reduplicated descriptive element built on میل ('inclination, tilt') used adverbially to say they lie/lean at angles against each other.
segments.s227-g122: به here introduces a quality or accompaniment ('with' / 'having on the face'), رخ is 'face/appearance', and چون is a simile marker 'like/as'. زریر is a proper name used as the point of comparison. The second half parallels the first: و links clauses, به لب 'with (regard to) the lip', همچو = 'like', and نیل refers to the dark-indigo color; the whole phrase gives parallel similes for face and lip.
segments.s227-g123: چنین + گفت is a formula meaning 'thus said'; the verb گفت is past-tense 'said' with the speaker gapped after it. گودرز is the subject (proper name). با + پیر سر addresses or identifies the addressee — با + person in older/epic usage functions like 'to' or 'with' when naming whom one speaks to; پیر سر is an epithet 'grey-/old-headed elder'.
segments.s227-g124: که introduces the content of what was said ('that' / 'that until...'). تا here means 'until' or 'so that' marking the purpose/timespan clause. من is the first‑person subject. ببستم is an old/formal verbal form (subjunctive/optative sense here) meaning 'I gird (or bind)'; in epic Persian forms with the b- prefix often render the verb in a volitive sense. بمردی is an adverbial/qualifying phrase 'with manliness' (i.e. 'manfully'), and کمر is the object/goal 'waist' or 'girdle' — together 'gird my loins with manliness.'
segments.s227-g125: ندیدم is the negative past form 'I did not see' (negated perfect/simple past). که introduces the clause content ('that' / 'that such a thing'). رزمی is an adjective/noun meaning 'martial' or 'pertaining to battle' and here modifies the implicit 'fight' or 'show of arms'. بود is the past copula 'was'. زین نشان is a compact phrase: زین functions as the demonstrative/poetic form ('this' / 'of this') and نشان is 'sign/mark' — 'of this sign' or 'of this kind.'
segments.s227-g126: نه هرگز is an emphatic negation 'never at all'. شنیدم is the past 'I heard' (here 'I heard of' / 'I was told'). ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' and گردنکشان is the agent 'the rebels' (literally 'neck‑pullers', i.e. proud or rebellious men). Together: 'nor have I ever heard of (this) from the rebels.'
segments.s227-g127: که again introduces a subordinate clause ('that'). از marks origin/reference 'from' or 'among'. کشته here is a substantive 'the slain' (those killed). گیتی is 'the world'. برین سان is a fixed poetic phrase meaning 'in this manner/like this' (بر + ین (this) + سان (kind/like)). بود is the past copula 'was'. The whole segment sets up that, among the world's slain, the situation ran thus.
segments.s227-g128: یکی ... و دیگر ... بود is a common binary pattern 'one ... and another ... was'. یکی is 'one' (one of them), خوار is an adjective 'despised/worthless'. و links the two members, دیگر 'the other/another', تن‌آسان is a compound adjective 'soft-bodied' or 'one who lives in ease/indulgence'. بود closes the copular clause 'was'. Together: '(among them) one was despised, another lived in ease.'
segments.s227-g41: This is a poetic, inverted word order: 'مرا' (me, accusative) and the prepositional phrase 'از دروغ تو' (of/about your lie) are fronted before the subject 'شاه جهان' (the king of the world). The implicit verb 'یاد کرد' (reminded / made remember) comes in the next segment; so here we have the object and its specifying prepositional phrase placed before the subject for emphasis.
segments.s227-g42: 'بسی' is an adverb of degree/frequency ('much/often') modifying the compound predicate 'یاد کرد' (past tense: 'reminded'). 'آشکار و نهان' are adverbial complements placed after the verb and mean 'openly and secretly' (publicly and in private); together the phrase says the king reminded the speaker repeatedly, both publicly and privately.
segments.s227-g43: 'وزان پس' is a temporal/connecting phrase 'and from that / after that.' Here 'کجا' functions rhetorically as an introductory interrogative/recording particle (roughly, 'what then / whereupon'), followed by the subject 'پیر گودرز' (the old Goudarz). 'گفت' is the past verb 'said'; the structure introduces reported speech that follows.
segments.s227-g44: 'همه' quantifies the coordinated noun phrase 'بند و نیرنگت' ('all (your) bonds and your trickery'); the possessive '-ت' is attached to نیرنگ marking 'your.' 'اندر نهفت' is a classical idiom meaning 'was hidden / lay concealed' (نهفت functioning as a past participle/adjectival predicate with اندر 'within'). Grammatically this is the reported content of what Goudarz said.
segments.s227-g45: بدیدم here is a classical/perfective form often used to mean 'I see / I perceive' or 'I have seen'; followed by the temporal adverb 'کنون' ('now'). The noun phrase 'دانش و رای تو' ('your knowledge and counsel') follows the verb, showing the typical classical word order where the verb can precede its object for emphasis; 'تو' is the possessive pronoun placed after the coordinated nouns.
segments.s227-g46: 'دروغست' is a predicative copular phrase 'is a lie' (دروغ + -ست). 'یکسر' is an adverb meaning 'entirely/utterly' modifying دروغست, and 'سراپای تو' ('your whole self / from head to foot') is a postposed nominal phrase intensifying total falsity. Together they assert that the previously named qualities are wholly false.
segments.s227-g47: بغلتی is a classical-form verb/adjective meaning 'you embrace / you hold to the bosom' (2nd-person sense in context); 'همی' is a classical continuous/emphatic marker (present/habitual flavor). 'خیره' is a predicative adjective 'staring / dazed.' The prepositional phrase 'در خون خویش' ('in your own blood') uses خویش + enclitic 'ش' to indicate possession/reflexive, so together this clause pictures the addressee held/staring amid their own blood.
segments.s227-g48: 'بدست' here functions as 'by/through' (introducing the source or cause), followed by the coordinated demonstratives 'این و زین' ('this and that') (classical 'زین' = 'زِ + این' meaning 'from that'); 'بدتر' is the comparative 'worse' and 'آیدت' is the impersonal/3rd-person-present-future 'will come' with enclitic object 'ت' ('will befall you'). 'پیش' is an adverb 'before / in store for,' so the clause predicts worse things will befall the addressee from both these sources.

sentences.s227-s49: Do not bind your heart inside a spongy house, for it will be filled with blood when necessity compels you.
sentences.s227-s50: If one has a companion whose spirit is wise, he will count the days of good and ill.
sentences.s227-s51: The Lord of the crown and the Lord of treasure does not bind the heart inside a spongy house.
sentences.s227-s52: Thus the army gave answer to Rostam, saying that your command is loftier than the sky and the moon.
sentences.s227-s29: A cloak of your own hide and leopard-skin pleases you more than colored brocade.
sentences.s227-s30: No one would make this judgment about you: that you would eat the seed you yourself scattered.
sentences.s227-s31: The elders said to him, 'O fortunate one, a noble, vigorous, and beautiful tree.'
sentences.s227-s32: Who but you knows such words? May praise from the elders be upon you.
sentences.s227-s13: An army came in this manner from China, from Sqalab and Khotlan and the land of Turan.
sentences.s227-s14: Where will Afrasiab make peace, since many armies have come from land and sea?
sentences.s227-s15: In reply to the reproach I received much reproach; thus I turned toward the champion.
sentences.s227-s16: And their host, like a sea of water, surged; they hastened to seek battle.
sentences.s227-s57: The spear-points spun about within; the spotless star cried, “Sing!”
sentences.s227-s58: Gavchehr’s mace was whirling about; you said the heavens rained stones.
sentences.s227-s59: Sing! — and into the very marrow within, thorn and dust; they became drowned, and their arms were rent, rent.
sentences.s227-s60: The whole plain was entirely full of streams of blood; in every place many lay thrown down and ruined.
sentences.s227-s45: I'll bind his two hands in the snare, even if he has the lofty heavens as an ally.
sentences.s227-s46: Do not all be comrades of a single bey; be not wearied because of those renowned men.
sentences.s227-s47: If the time comes for me to enter battle, I shall not die inside the hall, surely.
sentences.s227-s48: A name must endure long; do not try to survive by piling up many deeds.
sentences.s227-s1: From that spot he went to that assembly, to the place that lay in the shadow of Piltan.
sentences.s227-s2: He descended and gave some praise, saying that the high heaven might draw its strength from you.
sentences.s227-s3: May it not be that your day falls into decline; may a rebuke not come upon your face.
sentences.s227-s4: May the heart of the Shah of Iran be glad for you; may all your affairs be wholly (in) justice.
sentences.s227-s25: Such a life has no worth, for one's head would be in the dragon's breath.
sentences.s227-s26: Did I not say that that soil is oppressive and ill‑omened, and that you should not come to the inhabited land?
sentences.s227-s27: If you look, you will see the king, with justice and kindness, (surrounded by) youths, musicians, and fair‑faced ones.
sentences.s227-s28: He will take you as a father, without doubt, and will lift your head higher than the heavens.
sentences.s227-s5: I left your presence, O champion, and I delivered your message to both old and young.
sentences.s227-s6: I recounted whatever your skills were; I told people myself in the world that even a companion praised you.
sentences.s227-s7: I spoke both of reconciliation and of war; I spoke, without delay, on every topic.
sentences.s227-s8: At last they said, "How shall we accomplish this vengeance, so that we may drive hatred out of his counsel?"
sentences.s227-s37: Thus Rostam said to the Iranians, "I will keep the fighting contained between [us]."
sentences.s227-s38: You, appoint one bey whose head is full of hatred; make the warlike goers full of frowns (i.e. rouse grim, wrathful warriors).
sentences.s227-s39: For today a great battle lies before us; it will break out at the hour of dusk (the gray time between night and day).
sentences.s227-s40: That astronomer had told me; from that day my heart was filled with dread.
sentences.s227-s33: My life and heart are under your command; my spirit is always a hostage/pledge to you.
sentences.s227-s34: Tonight, for a while, I will take counsel with myself; I will also speak with the assembly.
sentences.s227-s35: And from there there came into (his) heart a black (evil) thing: a tongue full of lies and a vengeful spirit.
sentences.s227-s36: When the elders returned from both bands, the earth became like a mountain seething with deeds.
sentences.s227-s41: That there was a combat between two mountains, where the peoples of the world would there join in the same company.
sentences.s227-s42: A gathering of battle‑tested guests will form; because of that war the world will become bereft of men.
sentences.s227-s43: Following that hidden hatred there will arise (a movement) from the surface of the land; the steel mace will be as if turned to wax.
sentences.s227-s44: Whoever comes to fight against us — you, do not let your hearts be troubled by that tight affair.
sentences.s227-s53: Let us make such a battle with sharp swords that our name will remain until the Resurrection.
sentences.s227-s54: From two narrow approaches the host came in; a single dark cloud, as it were, rose up.
sentences.s227-s55: For whose rain was sword and arrow; the world became like a sea of tar.
sentences.s227-s56: By steel‑headed arrows and eagle‑feathers the sun's bright face turned black.
sentences.s227-s17: The king of Hind will indeed take up battle against you; with bows and arrows the Indians will rush forth.
sentences.s227-s18: I am sure of this: that because of Piltan the assembly will at last be in tears.
sentences.s227-s19: When Rostam heard (this) he became very angry; to the elders he thus spoke: 'O unlucky one...'
sentences.s227-s20: You, with such ties and many deceits, where will you have a footing on the day of rebuke?
sentences.s227-s9: We can give treasure and gold — and whatever he desires from us is furnished.
sentences.s227-s10: It is not fitting to pronounce someone guilty prematurely; reflect on it and investigate these secrets.
sentences.s227-s11: The guilty one is none other than Afrasiab himself; since you know the matter, do not state it in haste.
sentences.s227-s12: From among us, whomever he chooses are all chiefs; they are great and possess throne and helmet.
sentences.s227-s61: Like elephants thrown together, slanting against one another; with faces like Zarīr and with lips the color of indigo.
sentences.s227-s62: Thus Goudarz said to the grey‑headed elder: 'That until I gird my loins with manliness...'
sentences.s227-s63: I have not seen that any fighting was of this kind, nor have I ever heard of it from the rebels.
sentences.s227-s64: That among the slain of the world it was like this: one (was) despised and another was easy‑bodied (lived in ease).
sentences.s227-s21: The king of the world often reminded me of your lies, openly and in secret.
sentences.s227-s22: And after that what did the old man Goudarz say? — "All your bonds and trickery were concealed."
sentences.s227-s23: Now I see that your learning and counsel are lies outright — you are false from head to foot.
sentences.s227-s24: You will hold (him) to your bosom and stand dazed in your own blood; from this and from that worse will befall you.
